title: Stanford Cart Autonomously Navigates a Room of Obstacles
summary: Hans Moravec's Stanford Cart successfully navigated a chair-filled room autonomously using computer vision, becoming one of the first demonstrations of autonomous vehicle navigation. The cart used a single TV camera mounted on a sliding rail to capture stereo images, built 3D models of its environment, and planned obstacle-free paths. The journey across the 20-meter room took approximately 5 hours, as the cart stopped every meter to capture images and spend 10-15 minutes computing its next move. Despite its glacial pace, the Stanford Cart demonstrated that autonomous navigation using computer vision was possible.
impact_description: The Stanford Cart was a crucial proof of concept for autonomous navigation, demonstrating that a robot could perceive and navigate through an unstructured environment using vision alone. Though impossibly slow for practical use, it established the fundamental approach — sense, build world model, plan path, execute — that modern self-driving vehicles still follow. Moravec's work also led to his formulation of Moravec's Paradox: tasks easy for humans (perception, mobility) are hard for computers, while tasks hard for humans (chess, math) are easy.
counterfactual: Without the Stanford Cart, autonomous vehicle research might have lacked its foundational demonstration. The proof that vision-based autonomous navigation was possible, even at a glacial pace, motivated decades of subsequent robotics research. Moravec's Paradox, which emerged from this work, remains one of the most important insights in AI — explaining why ChatGPT can pass the bar exam but robots still struggle to fold laundry.
